text: The surgeon retracts the hiatus anteriorly and to the right, removing the station 111 lymph node along with the specimen. A clear dissection plane is established between the specimen and the pericardium, with all fibro-fatty tissue taken with the specimen. A distinct plane is visible between the specimen and the mediastinal pleura.

The surgeon clears the surgical field by dissecting the mesoesophagus from the left and right sides of the mediastinal pleura. The mesoesophagus is lifted anteriorly, creating a visible plane between it and the descending aorta.

The surgeon works on the right anterolateral surgical field, carefully dissecting periosteovisal tissue. The procedure involves creating three distinct surgical fields: right anterolateral, left anterolateral, and posterior, with lymph node basins being removed with the specimen from each area.

The surgeon separates the mesoesophagus along with the perisophageal tissue from the left side of the mediastinal pleura. The descending aorta is visible below the dissection area. The dissection continues towards the arch of the aorta, with efforts to maintain a dry surgical field for clear visualization of anatomical landmarks.

The surgical field reveals the pericardium anteriorly and the descending aorta posteriorly. The mesoesophageal tissue, along with lymph nodes, is being removed with the esophagus specimen. As the dissection approaches the arch of the aorta, branches of the left vagus nerve are encountered and excised. The autopulmonary window, containing soft tissue and lymph nodes, is identified as part of the specimen on the left side.

The surgeon uses monopolar scissors for sharp dissection to separate the mesoesophagus from the left mediastinal pleura, the downward arch of the aorta, and anteriorly from the main pulmonary trunk and pericardium. The main trunk of the left vagus nerve is cut, releasing the specimen. Similar steps are repeated on the right side, where the right vagus nerve is identified and cut, allowing access to the right station 109 lymph node basin, which is taken with the specimen.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes the station 107 subcranial lymph node, ensuring minimal bleeding to maintain a clear surgical field. The right bronchial artery is visible anteriorly, serving as a landmark for defining the right bronchus. The dissection proceeds flush to the esophagus.

The surgeon performs azygous lymph node clearance at station 109, the final lymph node station before initiating neck dissection. The inferior border of the left bronchus is visible beneath the station 109 lymph node. All mesoesophageal tissue is removed with the specimen during the transital esophagectomy.

The surgeon performs posterior dissection, revealing the azygos vein and thoracic duct running parallel on the right side of the surgical field. The descending aorta is visible on the left side. An avascular plane is identified between the prevascular fascia covering major vessels and the visceral fascia covering the specimen, guiding the dissection towards the thoracic inlet. The thoracic duct, embedded within posterior fat, serves as an important landmark.
